policy_statement: Implement programmes to raise awareness and promote sustainable production and consumption patterns to reduce GHG emissions.
mechanism_description: Implement programmes that raise awareness and promote sustainable production and consumption patterns, shifting behavior across sectors in ways that reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Information/behavior change programs → shifts in consumption/production choices → ↓ energy/material demand and/or ↓ high-carbon activities → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Public, business, and institutional decision-making is influenced via awareness campaigns, education, labeling guidance, and norm-setting to choose more sustainable products, services, and practices.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced demand for carbon-intensive goods/services and improved operational practices lower fossil fuel combustion and industrial/process emissions (and may also reduce upstream supply-chain emissions via decreased material throughput and waste).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in territorial and/or consumption-based GHG emissions across multiple sectors (energy, transport, waste, industry, food systems), depending on program reach and uptake.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced greenhouse gas emissions due to behavior-driven shifts toward lower-carbon consumption and production.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Programs primarily act through information and norm/behavior change that reduces high-emission activities and resource/energy demand, yielding ↓ GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While these programs can incidentally improve resource efficiency or resilience, the mechanism as defined explicitly targets emissions reduction via sustainable consumption/production, which is a mitigation outcome.  
3) Edge cases applied: If framed around reducing vulnerability (e.g., disaster preparedness) it would be adaptation; if focused narrowly on efficiency (same service, less energy/material) it could be resource efficiency; if focused on ecosystem stewardship it could be nature-based solutions. Those are not the dominant or defining pathway in this mechanism.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that fits “sustainability awareness programs → consumption/production emissions reduction” has its principal climate effect through reduced emissions from changed choices and practices, so Mitigation applies across instances.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Specific sectoral subtypes (energy, waste, food, transport) and whether impacts are direct vs. supply-chain are policy-evidence dependent and should be assigned at the individual policy level, not at this mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Awareness programs can be multi-objective and sometimes primarily target resource conservation, health, or adaptation outcomes; classification should remain Mitigation here only when the dominant intended effect is emissions reduction through changed consumption/production patterns (not primarily efficiency accounting, resilience preparedness, or ecosystem restoration).
key_indicators: “implement programmes”; “raise awareness”; “impact of patterns of production and consumption”; “promote patterns of sustainable production and consumption”; “civil, social and private sectors”
co_benefits: Reduced waste and local air pollution; improved public health and resource efficiency
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a broader planning/strategy instrument, but the dominant mechanism is awareness/education (information-based) rather than formal planning requirements or targets.
